paragraphs.text: I grew up with video games – I watched my understanding of their evening on a small desktop computer in the 1990s. So much to sentiment (videos are part of my life since the raspberry). I have seen the last decade as consumers are increasingly ‘exploited’. Prices are higher, profits and assets and the ‘value’ of audiences are increasing, but customer rights and access to games/software are becoming more and more limited. I personally dealt with this frustration through sellers selling “DRM-free” games with this approach – I am satisfied – mostly offline installers, and there the whole problem hashn. And they are not just small developers and audiences, of which some CD Projekt Red, Larian Studios, Paradox, Devolver, Supergiant, Slitherine, and others can be appointed. So here we have the argument that DRM free also works. But I want to say mainly one: are adults buying our toys!... or do we lend (free of charge), don’t know the parent who rents the toys? Around last year, the state in the US California issued a law, a style that their citizens must be informed that they ‘do not only buy a video game’, a licence. But why we tolerate our children/young people from being exploited through unfair practices – ingame microtransactions, gacha games, gambling lootboxes with random loot inside a random value and addiction (on dopamine, expectation: now I am something good!not owning and game you buy (how serious?why should anyone ‘buy’?), always online, despite the fact that it is a single player? These practices were created because we allowed it. It’s understandable, it’s something new, a new market, new opportunities. But we now see the huge profits of this unregulated market (last years already exceeding film and music business combined) and the need to protect the customer from arbitrariness and exploitation. At least basic rights should be given back to customers. The right to own a purchased product (or a purchased licence for own use, not to pire) Although fear of piracy is just a pretext for oppressing and violating consumer rights. Let’s try to ask another question from another area: if the trader is afraid to steal his car. Does it install a camera/some ‘DRM’ there and if I don’t like to dissuade the speed I take my car and no longer gets my money back? Because this is now “working” with video games. The developer/publisher has all power and control in the hands of the developer/publisher, often no customer. Despite paying big money for video games (thus I think more than 40! My small brother has already spent 300 in one game and is not ‘difficult’ because it is a living service to which he or she does not remain a hard disk copy when the support ends.
